resume:
Marcus Delaney
Account Executive, Mid-Market SaaS | Denver, CO | marcus.delaney@email.com | (720) 555-0143

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Mid-market account executive with seven years carrying a full-cycle quota in B2B software. I have sold horizontal collaboration tooling, vertical fintech compliance platforms, and most recently a usage-based observability product. I am comfortable owning the entire motion from cold outbound through procurement and security review, and I have a consistent habit of building multi-threaded relationships rather than relying on a single champion. I describe my style as discovery-heavy and slow to discount. I have never been the top rep on a board of twenty, but I have also never missed two consecutive quarters, and managers tend to keep me because my forecast is boring and accurate.

EXPERIENCE

LumaTrace (observability SaaS) — Senior Account Executive, Mid-Market
April 2022 to present, Denver
LumaTrace sells a usage-based logging and tracing platform to engineering orgs between 200 and 2,000 employees. Deals land between 35k and 180k ACV with a median of about 62k. The sales cycle runs 70 to 110 days because there is almost always a security review and a proof-of-value where the prospect ships real traffic through our collectors for two weeks.
- Carried a 1.4M annual quota across the West region. Closed at 104% of plan in FY23 and was at 88% pacing through Q3 of FY24 when a large renewal slipped a quarter.
- Ran roughly 40 active opportunities at any time, with an average of 3.2 stakeholders engaged per closed-won deal.
- Built the team's first reusable POV scoping checklist after watching three proofs-of-value stall because nobody had defined success criteria up front. After the checklist, our POV-to-close rate on the West team went from a little under half to about two-thirds over two quarters.
- Partnered closely with a solutions engineer named Priya on every technical evaluation; I handled commercial and procurement, she handled the architecture mapping.
- Lost a marquee 150k deal to a competitor late in the cycle when their VP of Engineering changed and the new VP had a prior relationship with the incumbent vendor. I documented the loss honestly in the post-mortem rather than blaming product.

Fernweh Compliance (regtech SaaS) — Account Executive
June 2019 to March 2022, Denver and remote
Fernweh sold a transaction-monitoring and KYC workflow product to regional banks and credit unions. Smaller logos, longer cycles, heavier legal involvement.
- Carried an 850k quota. Hit 96%, 112%, and 91% across my three full years.
- Closed the company's first credit-union deal, which required learning the specific examination language that NCUA-regulated institutions care about. That logo became a reference account that marketing used in two case studies.
- Sat through long procurement cycles where legal redlines could add six weeks; learned to involve our counsel early instead of treating the MSA as a final-step formality.
- Mentored two SDRs on discovery call structure. One was promoted to AE.

Brightroll Collaboration (horizontal SaaS) — Sales Development Rep, then Junior AE
August 2017 to May 2019, Boulder
Started in a pooled SDR role booking meetings for the enterprise team, then moved into a junior closing seat for sub-15k transactional deals.
- Booked 18 to 22 qualified meetings a month as an SDR, consistently top third of the pod.
- As a junior AE, closed about 30 small deals in my first year with a short, high-velocity cycle.
- Learned the hard way that spraying generic outbound burns territory; rebuilt my sequences around a single named pain per persona.

WHAT I AM GOOD AT
- Discovery. I ask a lot of questions and I am not afraid of silence on a call. My champions tend to say I understood their problem better than the last vendor did.
- Multi-threading. I deliberately try to meet the economic buyer, the technical evaluator, and at least one skeptic before I forecast a deal as commit.
- Honest forecasting. I would rather sandbag a quarter than blow up a manager's roll-up. I move deals to commit slowly.
- Procurement and security navigation. I have been through enough SOC 2 questionnaires and redline cycles that they no longer scare me.

WHAT I AM STILL WORKING ON
- Outbound volume. My self-sourced pipeline is healthy but not enormous; I lean on marketing-sourced leads more than I would like.
- Negotiating without discounting. I default to defending price, but I know I sometimes leave the trade — like a longer term or a case-study commitment — on the table.
- Executive presence on first calls with a CFO. I am strong with practitioners and directors, less polished with the C-suite.

TOOLS
Salesforce, Outreach, Gong, Clari for forecasting, Notion for account plans. I actually use Gong call reviews to coach myself, not just because it is mandated.

EDUCATION
B.A. Economics, University of Colorado Boulder, 2017.

A NOTE ON HOW I WORK
I am not a flashy rep. I am the person who reads the 10-K before a discovery call with a public company, who writes a real mutual action plan, and who tells a manager the truth about a slipping deal in week three instead of week eleven. I am looking for a mid-market seat where discovery and multi-threading are rewarded and where I can finally tighten up my self-sourced outbound with a team that does it well.
jd:
Mid-Market Account Executive — Helix Data Platform (Series C, ~400 employees)

Helix sells a real-time data-integration platform to product and data teams. We are hiring a mid-market AE to own net-new logos in the 250 to 1,500 employee segment with ACVs from 40k to 200k. You will run a full-cycle motion: self-sourced and BDR-sourced pipeline, technical evaluation alongside a dedicated solutions engineer, procurement, and security review. Expected sales cycle is 60 to 100 days. Quota is 1.3M with accelerators above plan.

What we need to see:
- A repeatable, self-sourced outbound engine — not just inbound-fed pipeline. We will ask you to walk us through a specific territory you built from scratch.
- Disciplined discovery and multi-threading. We sell to skeptical engineering buyers; a single champion is not enough.
- Command of your numbers: win rate, average cycle length, stage conversion, and how you forecast.
- Negotiation that protects price through value, term, and concessions other than raw discount.
- A growth mindset about a documented weakness and concrete steps you took to close the gap.

This is an in-person-flexible role in Denver. You will report to the West Regional Director and partner daily with a solutions engineer and a BDR.

history.q: Thanks for making time, Marcus. Give me the thirty-second version of your current role at LumaTrace and the number you carry.
You closed at 104% in FY23. What share of that pipeline did you source yourself versus marketing or BDR?
Walk me through one specific deal you self-sourced from a cold start. Who did you target and why?
What was your win rate on self-sourced opportunities specifically, separate from inbound?
Tell me about the POV scoping checklist you built. What problem were you solving and what changed after?
You said POV-to-close went from under half to about two-thirds. How did you actually measure that, and over how many deals?
Let's talk multi-threading. On that lost 150k deal, how many stakeholders did you have, and where did the threading break?
Your resume lists 'negotiating without discounting' as a growth area. Tell me about a recent deal where you protected price.
In that deal, what specifically did you trade instead of cutting price — term, references, scope?
How do you decide when a deal moves from best-case to commit in your forecast?
Give me an example of a deal you sandbagged that then came in early. What signal did you under-weight?
You mentioned executive presence with CFOs is a gap. What concrete step have you taken in the last six months to improve there?
Walk me through how you'd build a brand-new territory here at Helix in your first 60 days — specific accounts, specific motion.
Last one on outbound: in your current seat, how many self-sourced meetings do you book in a typical month, and through what channel?
Let me bring it back to the territory-building question. Concretely, what is the first cohort of accounts you'd go after at Helix, and what's your outbound sequence for them?

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, so honestly what really energizes me about Helix is the culture and the mission — real-time data is just such an exciting space right now, and I love that you're a Series C company with that scrappy energy. I think the market timing is incredible. I've been reading a lot about how the data-integration category is heating up, and there was this great podcast last week about the modern data stack that totally changed how I think about the buyer. I'm a huge believer in being a student of the market. And the team I met in the first round seemed really sharp, so I just feel like the fit is there.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'm a huge believer in being a student of the market

expected-yield: redirect off the culture-and-market enthusiasm back to the unanswered question — a concrete first-60-days territory plan with named target segments or account archetypes, the prioritization logic, and the specific outbound sequence and channels he would run
